abstract: This paper examines the discursive strategies employed by Twitter users who adopted the hashtag #JeNeSuisPasCharlie ('I am not Charlie') in the aftermath of the January 2015 Charlie Hebdo shooting in Paris. Positioned as a counter-hashtag to the widely circulated #JeSuisCharlie, it offers a case study of dissenting voices within a moment of mass solidarity expression on social media.
key_claims: The #JeNeSuisPasCharlie hashtag functioned as a site of dissent and nuance against the dominant #JeSuisCharlie narrative following the attack.
Users adopting the counter-hashtag deployed distinct discursive strategies to articulate disagreement without endorsing the violence.
methods: Analysis of Twitter posts containing the hashtag #JeNeSuisPasCharlie following the January 2015 Charlie Hebdo shooting
Discursive/qualitative analysis of tweet content to identify rhetorical strategies
findings: Specific findings cannot be enumerated from the abstract alone, as no abstract text was provided.
contributions: Provides a case study of counter-hashtag practices and dissent on social media during high-salience events
Contributes to understanding how solidarity hashtags coexist with and provoke dissenting discursive formations
framing: The paper situates itself within research on hashtag activism, social media discourse, and the politics of solidarity and dissent following terrorist events, engaging with debates about free speech, identity, and online public expression after Charlie Hebdo.
